research.maturity: Shipped and real as of early 2026, but a young, lightly-versioned standard rather than a frozen formal spec. The spec is published at agentskills.io/specification with concrete, enforceable field constraints, and is backed by github.com/agentskills/agentskills (Apache-2.0 code, CC-BY-4.0 docs; ~123 commits; community CONTRIBUTING). Maturity caveats: the GitHub repo shows NO formal version/release tags (no v1.0) — the spec is "actively maintained" with no numbered version; allowed-tools is explicitly marked EXPERIMENTAL; the skills-ref library is explicitly demonstration-only, not production. Adoption is genuinely broad and shipped — the /clients showcase lists ~40+ real products (Claude Code, Claude API, Cursor, VS Code/GitHub Copilot, Gemini CLI, OpenAI Codex, Goose, OpenCode, OpenHands, Roo Code, Letta, Factory, Kiro, Tabnine, Laravel Boost, Spring AI, and more), most with live skills docs. So: a real, working, multi-vendor de-facto standard with strong momentum, but governance/versioning is still informal and some fields are experimental.
research.skillFormat:
A skill is a directory whose name matches the skill's `name`. Required file: SKILL.md (exact filename), containing YAML frontmatter between `---` delimiters followed by a Markdown body. Frontmatter fields (per https://agentskills.io/specification):
- name (REQUIRED): 1-64 chars; lowercase unicode alphanumeric (a-z, 0-9) and hyphens only; must not start/end with a hyphen; no consecutive hyphens (`--`); must match the parent directory name.
- description (REQUIRED): 1-1024 chars, non-empty; should state what the skill does AND when to use it, with keywords to aid matching.
- license (optional): license name or reference to a bundled license file (e.g. "Apache-2.0", "Proprietary. LICENSE.txt has complete terms").
- compatibility (optional): max 500 chars; environment requirements (intended product, system packages, network access, runtime versions). Most skills omit it.
- metadata (optional): arbitrary string-key→string-value map for client-specific extras (commonly holds author, version); spec recommends unique key names to avoid collisions.
- allowed-tools (optional, EXPERIMENTAL): space-separated string of pre-approved tools, e.g. `Bash(git:*) Bash(jq:*) Read`; support varies by implementation.
Note: there is no spec-defined top-level `version` field — version is conventionally placed under `metadata.version`.
Body: free-form Markdown instructions (recommended <500 lines / <5000 tokens); split overflow into referenced files.
Optional directories (all sibling to SKILL.md):
- scripts/ — executable code (Python/Bash/JS common; language support depends on the agent)
- references/ — on-demand docs (e.g. REFERENCE.md, domain files)
- assets/ — templates, images, data files/schemas
File references use relative paths from the skill root, kept ~one level deep (e.g. `scripts/extract.py`, `references/REFERENCE.md`).
research.discovery:
The spec defines only what goes INSIDE a skill directory, not where directories live; discovery conventions are documented in the client-implementation guide (https://agentskills.io/client-implementation/adding-skills-support). A locally-running agent scans the filesystem at session start for subdirectories containing a file named exactly SKILL.md, typically at two scopes — project-level (relative to working dir) and user-level (relative to home) — and within each scope scans both a client-specific dir and the cross-client convention:
- <project>/.<client>/skills/ and <project>/.agents/skills/
- ~/.<client>/skills/ and ~/.agents/skills/
`.agents/skills/` is the emergent cross-client interop convention; many runtimes also scan `.claude/skills/` for compatibility, plus ancestor dirs to git root (monorepos), XDG dirs, and user-configured paths. Scanning rules: skip .git/node_modules, optionally respect .gitignore, bound depth (~4-6) and dir count (~2000). Name collisions resolve by deterministic precedence — project-level overrides user-level (universal convention) — with a warning logged on shadowing. Project-level skills should be trust-gated (untrusted cloned repos can inject instructions). Cloud/sandboxed agents that lack the user filesystem provision skills via an API, remote registry, cloned config repo, uploaded directories, or static built-in assets. Parsing is lenient: warn-but-load on name mismatches/over-length; skip only when description is missing/empty or YAML is unparseable; a common fallback quotes unquoted colon values in descriptions.
research.interoperability: Direct 1:1 mapping to Anthropic Agent Skills — they are the SAME standard. The format was originally developed by Anthropic, open-sourced, and agentskills.io is the vendor-neutral home for that spec; Anthropic's own SKILL.md (Claude Code / Claude API) is a conforming implementation, as are Cursor, VS Code/Copilot, Gemini CLI, OpenAI Codex, Goose, OpenCode, OpenHands, Roo Code, etc. No adapter is needed across these clients beyond differing default scan paths (.claude/skills/ vs .agents/skills/ vs client-specific) and minor activation-mechanism differences; the .agents/skills/ convention exists specifically to make skills installed by one compliant client visible to others. Relationship to MCP is COMPLEMENTARY, not equivalent: Agent Skills package procedural knowledge/instructions + files loaded into context via progressive disclosure (no protocol, no server, no wire format), whereas MCP is a client-server JSON-RPC protocol exposing tools/resources/prompts at runtime. A skill can reference or use MCP tools (and allowed-tools can pre-approve them), but the two solve different problems — skills = "what/how to do it" as portable Markdown; MCP = "live capability/tool access" over a connection. Adapter feasibility for anymodel: trivial to consume on disk (parse YAML frontmatter + Markdown), since it is just files; the work is in the runtime-side progressive-disclosure plumbing, not in any format translation.
research.gapsForAnymodel:
To support Agent Skills natively, anymodel would need to build the runtime-side progressive-disclosure pipeline (the format itself is trivial — plain directories + YAML+Markdown):
1. Discovery/scanner: walk conventional paths at project + user scope — at minimum .agents/skills/ (cross-client interop) plus .claude/skills/ for ecosystem compatibility, with bounded depth/count, .git/node_modules skipping, optional .gitignore respect.
2. Lenient parser: split YAML frontmatter from Markdown body; extract name/description (+optional license/compatibility/metadata/allowed-tools); warn-but-load on name/dir mismatch or over-length; skip only on missing description or unparseable YAML; include the unquoted-colon fallback for cross-client description compatibility.
3. Catalog injection (tier 1): build a compact name+description(+location) catalog into the system prompt or an activation-tool description; hide disabled/denied skills.
4. Activation (tier 2): support BOTH model-driven file-read activation and (recommended) a dedicated activate_skill tool with a name enum to block hallucinated names; decide frontmatter-strip vs full-file; structured wrapping (<skill_content>) for later identification; plus user-explicit /skill-name activation.
5. Resource handling (tier 3): on-demand loading of scripts/references/assets, directory allowlisting to suppress permission prompts, and sandboxed execution for scripts (Python/Bash/JS).
6. Context lifecycle: exempt activated skill content from compaction/pruning; dedupe re-activations; optional subagent delegation.
7. Collision precedence (project overrides user) with warnings; project-level trust gating.
8. For cloud/sandboxed deployment: an alternative provisioning path (API/remote registry/uploaded dirs/built-in assets) since the user filesystem is unavailable.
9. Optionally vendor or reimplement skills-ref-style validation (`validate`) — but treat skills-ref as a demo, not a dependency.
Note: there is no official package format or central registry to integrate; distribution is git/filesystem, and any "marketplace" (skillstore.io, block/agent-skills, anthropics/skills) is a separate third-party source anymodel would choose to support independently.

verification.verdict:
CONFIRMED. Every concrete, refutable claim in this research was independently verified against live sources and matched — there are no hallucinated file formats, frontmatter fields, manifest schemas, or protocol names.

VERIFIED AGAINST LIVE SOURCES:
• agentskills.io exists and is a Mintlify spec+docs site (NOT a registry) — confirmed /home, /specification, /clients, /client-implementation/adding-skills-support, and all five /skill-creation pages (quickstart, best-practices, evaluating-skills, optimizing-descriptions, using-scripts) via the live llms.txt index. No registry/marketplace page exists in the index, as claimed.
• SKILL.md format spec — EXACT match. Required: name (max 64 chars, 1-64, lowercase a-z/0-9/hyphens, no leading/trailing hyphen, no consecutive '--', must match parent dir) and description (1-1024 chars, non-empty). Optional: license, compatibility (max 500 chars), metadata (string key→value map), allowed-tools (space-separated, explicitly marked EXPERIMENTAL, example 'Bash(git:*) Bash(jq:*) Read'). Confirmed there is NO top-level version field — version goes under metadata.version (the live spec's own example shows exactly `metadata: { version: "1.0" }`). Body <500 lines / <5000 tokens recommended. Optional scripts/, references/, assets/ dirs all confirmed.
• Three-tier progressive disclosure — EXACT match including the live table: Catalog (name+description, session start, ~50-100 tokens/skill), Instructions (full SKILL.md body, <5000 tokens recommended), Resources (on demand).
• Discovery conventions — EXACT match. <project>/.<client>/skills/, <project>/.agents/skills/, ~/.<client>/skills/, ~/.agents/skills/; .agents/skills/ as the cross-client interop convention; .claude/skills/ scanned 'for pragmatic compatibility'; project-over-user collision precedence; trust-gating untrusted repos; lenient parsing (warn-but-load on name mismatch/over-length, skip on missing description or unparseable YAML, unquoted-colon fallback); dedicated activate_skill tool with name-enum to block hallucinated names; <skill_content> structured wrapping; cloud provisioning via API/registry/uploads/built-in assets — all present verbatim.
• skills-ref — confirmed Python library+CLI with validate, read-properties, to-prompt; generates <available_skills> block; explicitly 'intended for demonstration purposes only... not meant to be used in production'.
• github.com/agentskills/agentskills — confirmed Apache-2.0 (code) + CC-BY-4.0 (docs), ~123 commits, CONTRIBUTING.md present, NO release/version tags (no v1.0), README states Anthropic origin verbatim.
• Anthropic origin + 1:1 mapping — confirmed: live /home says 'originally developed by Anthropic, released as an open standard'; Claude platform docs are listed as a conforming client. Broad multi-vendor adoption confirmed (Claude Code, Cursor, VS Code/Copilot, Gemini CLI, OpenAI Codex, Goose, OpenCode, OpenHands, Roo Code, Letta, Factory, Kiro, Tabnine, Laravel Boost, Spring AI + ~30 more on the live /clients carousel).
• Third-party marketplaces — both skillstore.io and agentskills.to verified as real and distinct from agentskills.io; anthropics/skills verified as Anthropic's public skill collection (~146k stars). The research's claim that catalogs/marketplaces are separate third-party properties holds.

The anymodel takeaway (format is trivial plain dirs + YAML/Markdown; the real work is runtime-side progressive-disclosure plumbing) is sound and well-supported. Only nitpicks: a cosmetic skillstore.io-vs-agentskills.to naming mismatch between the summary and the cited source (both are real, so the claim survives), and the harmless token-count phrasing difference between two doc pages. Hallucination risk: none.

sources.claim: agentskills.io is the home/overview site for Agent Skills: a lightweight open format where a skill is a folder containing SKILL.md (metadata + instructions), optionally bundling scripts/references/assets; works via progressive disclosure (discovery/activation/execution); originally developed by Anthropic and released as an open standard; adopted by 40+ listed agent products.

Full format spec: required name (1-64 chars, lowercase a-z/0-9/hyphens, no leading/trailing or consecutive hyphens, must match parent dir) and description (1-1024 chars); optional license, compatibility (max 500), metadata (string map), allowed-tools (space-separated, experimental); SKILL.md = YAML frontmatter + Markdown body (<500 lines / <5000 tokens recommended); optional scripts/, references/, assets/ dirs; validate via skills-ref.

Runtime consumption guide: 3-tier progressive disclosure (catalog ~50-100 tokens, instructions <5000 tokens, resources on demand); scan project+user scopes at .<client>/skills/ and .agents/skills/ (plus .claude/skills/ for compat) for subdirs containing SKILL.md; lenient YAML parsing; model-driven activation via file-read or dedicated activate_skill tool; project-over-user collision precedence; trust gating; cloud agents provision via API/registry/uploads.

Documentation index lists pages: /home, /specification, /clients, /client-implementation/adding-skills-support, and /skill-creation/{quickstart,best-practices,evaluating-skills,optimizing-descriptions,using-scripts}. No registry/marketplace page is present in the index.

GitHub repo is the specification + documentation home; Apache-2.0 (code) and CC-BY-4.0 (docs); community CONTRIBUTING; originally from Anthropic; ~123 commits and NO formal version/release tags (no numbered spec version).

skills-ref is a Python reference library + CLI (commands include validate, read-properties, to-prompt) that generates <available_skills> prompt blocks; explicitly for demonstration, not production use.
Anthropic's Claude platform documents Agent Skills as its own SKILL.md implementation, confirming agentskills.io describes the same standard Anthropic originated (1:1 mapping, not a separate format).

agentskills.to is a separate third-party 'Agent Skills Marketplace' — distinct from agentskills.io (the spec site) — indicating registries/marketplaces are external properties, not part of agentskills.io itself.
anthropics/skills is Anthropic's public repository of Agent Skills, a separate source of actual skill content distinct from the agentskills.io spec repo.

research.maturity: Shipped and stable as of early 2026, with rapidly growing cross-vendor adoption. Timeline: introduced in Anthropic's engineering blog "Equipping agents for the real world with Agent Skills" on Oct 16, 2025; the open SKILL.md format, spec, and `skills-ref` validator are live at agentskills.io and github.com/agentskills/agentskills (code Apache-2.0, docs CC-BY-4.0); Anthropic formally published Agent Skills as an open standard on Dec 18, 2025 for cross-platform portability. Real, shipped consumers exist across Claude Code, claude.ai, and the Claude API (the API path is still gated behind beta headers, so that surface is beta-flagged, not GA). Multiple third-party agents document native support. CAVEATS on the breathless secondary coverage: aggregator articles (financialcontent, Unite.AI, VentureBeat) claim the standard was "donated to the Agentic AI Foundation (Linux Foundation-backed)" and that "OpenAI/Microsoft/Google adopted it" — I could NOT confirm the foundation donation from a primary/strong source (SiliconANGLE's Dec 18 piece notes only that the *separate* MCP protocol went to the Linux Foundation, and lists Agent Skills launch *partners* as Atlassian, Canva, Notion, Figma, Cloudflare, Stripe, Zapier — not a governance handoff). Treat the "AAIF/Linux Foundation governance" claim as unverified. The spec itself does not advertise an explicit version number. Bottom line: the format and the open-standard publication are confirmed real; the precise governance body and the full breadth of "Big-3 lab adoption" are partly press-amplified and not fully verifiable from primary sources.
research.skillFormat: A skill is a directory whose name matches the skill `name`, containing a required `SKILL.md` (YAML frontmatter delimited by `---`, then a Markdown body). agentskills.io spec frontmatter fields: `name` (REQUIRED; 1-64 chars; lowercase a-z/0-9/hyphens only; must not start/end with hyphen; no consecutive `--`; must match parent directory name). `description` (REQUIRED; 1-1024 chars; non-empty; should state both what the skill does and when to use it, with trigger keywords). `license` (optional; license name or reference to a bundled license file). `compatibility` (optional; 1-500 chars; environment requirements e.g. intended product, system packages, network access). `metadata` (optional; arbitrary string→string key-value map for client-specific data not defined by the spec). `allowed-tools` (optional, marked EXPERIMENTAL; space-separated string of pre-approved tools, e.g. `Bash(git:*) Bash(jq:*) Read`). Note: the base spec does NOT define a top-level `version` field — versioning is done via `metadata.version`. The Claude *platform/API* docs list only `name` and `description` as the required/supported fields and explicitly forbid `name` containing reserved words "anthropic"/"claude" or XML tags (a Claude-specific constraint, not in the open spec). Claude *Code* extends frontmatter well beyond the open spec with optional fields including: `when_to_use`, `argument-hint`, `arguments`, `disable-model-invocation`, `user-invocable`, `allowed-tools`, `disallowed-tools`, `model`, `effort`, `context` (`fork`), `agent`, `hooks`, `paths`, `shell`. Directory layout (spec): `skill-name/SKILL.md` (required) + optional `scripts/` (executable code), `references/` (on-demand docs like REFERENCE.md/FORMS.md), `assets/` (templates/images/data). File references should be relative paths, one level deep. Recommended limits: SKILL.md body under 500 lines / <5k tokens.
research.discovery: Filesystem-based discovery. At agent startup, only each skill's `name` + `description` (~100 tokens each, "Level 1" metadata) are loaded into the system prompt so the agent knows the skill exists and roughly when it applies, without loading bodies. In Claude Code, skills are discovered from layered directories: enterprise/managed settings, personal `~/.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md`, project `.claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (loaded from the start dir and every parent up to repo root, plus nested dirs on demand for monorepos), and plugin `<plugin>/skills/<name>/SKILL.md` (namespaced `plugin:skill`). Precedence: enterprise > personal > project; plugin skills are namespaced so they don't collide. Legacy `.claude/commands/*.md` files are also discovered and treated as skills. Claude Code watches skill dirs for live changes within a session (new top-level dirs need a restart). The platform/API surface instead registers skills via the `/v1/skills` endpoints and references them by `skill_id` in the `container` param (workspace-wide sharing); claude.ai uploads skills as zip files per-user. Skills do NOT sync across these surfaces. When many skills exist, descriptions are truncated to a character budget (default ~1% of context window; per-entry cap 1,536 chars in Claude Code) — least-used skills' descriptions are dropped first.
research.interoperability: Strong, direct mapping — Agent Skills IS the open, portable layer, deliberately positioned as complementary to MCP rather than overlapping. Anthropic's blog states Skills "complement Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers by teaching agents more complex workflows that involve external tools and software": MCP standardizes how an agent connects to external tools/data sources (the wiring), while a Skill is procedural know-how (the playbook) that can instruct the agent to use those MCP tools. They compose cleanly; neither replaces the other. Portability is real and verifiable: agentskills.io publishes a vendor-neutral spec, a `skills-ref` validator, and a Client Showcase of independent adopters each linking their own skills docs — including Cursor, GitHub Copilot, VS Code, OpenAI Codex, Google Gemini CLI, Goose (Block), OpenCode, OpenHands, Roo Code, Letta, Factory, Kiro, and others. Claude Code, claude.ai, and the Claude API all consume the same SKILL.md format. Adapter feasibility for a system like anymodel is HIGH: SKILL.md is plain Markdown + a tiny YAML header with only two required fields and stable validation rules, so a parser + progressive-disclosure loader is straightforward. Key portability caveats: (a) the base spec's required surface is just `name`+`description`; everything else (`allowed-tools` is "experimental", plus all Claude Code extensions) is implementation-defined, so portable skills should rely only on `name`/`description`/`license`/`metadata`; (b) Claude's reserved-word ban on "anthropic"/"claude" in `name` is Claude-specific; (c) `metadata` is the spec-sanctioned escape hatch for custom fields (e.g. version, tags) so an adapter can carry extra data without breaking other consumers.
research.gapsForAnymodel: To support Agent Skills natively, anymodel would need: (1) A SKILL.md parser — read YAML frontmatter, validate `name` (1-64, lowercase/digits/hyphens, no leading/trailing/double hyphen, matches dir name) and `description` (1-1024, non-empty); tolerate optional `license`/`compatibility`/`metadata`/`allowed-tools`; ignore unknown Claude-Code-specific fields gracefully. The `skills-ref` library (github.com/agentskills/agentskills) is the canonical validator to mirror/wrap. (2) A discovery + indexer that scans skill directories (mirror Claude Code's layering: user dir, project `.claude/skills/`, parent dirs to repo root, nested dirs on demand) and loads ONLY name+description into the system prompt at startup, respecting a character/token budget with eviction of least-used descriptions. (3) A progressive-disclosure loader: on description-match (or explicit invocation), inject the full SKILL.md body; resolve relative file references and load `references/`/`assets/` on demand; execute `scripts/` via the sandbox/bash and feed back only stdout (not source) to preserve context economy — this REQUIRES a filesystem + code-execution capability in the runtime, which is the main architectural prerequisite. (4) A triggering/matching policy: decide automatic activation by semantic match of the request against `description`/`when_to_use`, plus an explicit `/skill-name`-style invocation path. (5) MCP composition: since Skills are designed to drive MCP tools, the loader should run alongside anymodel's MCP client so a skill can reference MCP tools by name. (6) Optional Claude-Code-compat layer if targeting existing skills in the wild: support `disable-model-invocation`, `allowed-tools`/`disallowed-tools`, `$ARGUMENTS`/`$N` substitution, and `!`cmd`` dynamic shell injection. (7) Decide a per-surface sharing/persistence model (skills don't sync across Claude surfaces today; anymodel can define its own). Minimal viable adapter = items 1-4; full parity = +5-7.

corrections: No corrections to the core format/discovery claims — all verified verbatim against primary sources. The following are clarifications/nuances, not errors: (1) On Claude *Code* (code.claude.com), the frontmatter `name` field is documented as OPTIONAL and is a display label that defaults to the directory name; on the Claude *platform/API* (platform.claude.com) `name` is REQUIRED. The research states both correctly, but a reader should not conflate the two surfaces' requiredness of `name`.

(2) The research correctly self-flags the contested claims. Confirmed via SiliconANGLE: Agent Skills was NOT donated to the 'Agentic AI Foundation' or Linux Foundation — only the SEPARATE MCP protocol was handed to the Linux Foundation. The aggregator (financialcontent) claims of foundation donation and OpenAI/Microsoft/Google *adoption* remain UNVERIFIED; SiliconANGLE makes no Big-3-lab adoption claim and even quotes analyst skepticism. The research's 'low confidence' / 'unverified' treatment of these is the correct call.

(3) Launch partners are confirmed as Atlassian, Canva, Notion, Figma, Cloudflare, Stripe, Zapier (VentureBeat additionally lists Vercel). The cross-vendor *adopter* showcase on agentskills.io confirms Cursor, GitHub Copilot, VS Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini CLI, Goose, OpenCode, OpenHands, Roo Code, Letta, Factory, and Kiro all present — note these are independent client adopters, distinct from the enterprise skill-building launch partners; both lists check out.
verification.verdict: CONFIRMED. Every concrete, refutable claim (SKILL.md format, frontmatter schema with exact field constraints, directory layout, progressive-disclosure token budgets, skills-ref validator, Apache-2.0/CC-BY-4.0 licensing, Oct 16 2025 blog date, Dec 18 2025 open-standard publication, API beta-header strings, skill_id/container/v1/skills API surface, Claude Code's extended frontmatter fields and !`cmd` injection, cross-vendor adopter list) was independently verified against live primary sources: agentskills.io/specification, github.com/agentskills/agentskills, platform.claude.com docs, code.claude.com/docs/en/skills, the Anthropic engineering blog, and SiliconANGLE. No hallucinated file formats, invented frontmatter fields, or fabricated protocol/standard names were found — a notable feat given the surface area of specific claims. The frontmatter table, name/description regex rules, the explicit absence of a top-level `version` field (versioning via metadata.version), the Experimental marking of allowed-tools, and the ~100/<5000-token disclosure tiers all match the published spec verbatim. The only soft spots in the research are the ones the author already flagged as unverified (the Agentic-AI-Foundation/Linux-Foundation governance handoff and the breadth of Big-3-lab adoption), which my checks confirm are NOT supported by primary sources — the MCP protocol went to the Linux Foundation, not Agent Skills. Because those are correctly self-quarantined as low-confidence rather than asserted as fact, the core research stands.

research.maturity: SHIPPED/real: (1) Agent Skills (SKILL.md) as an open standard at agentskills.io with a published spec and reference validator (skills-ref) — real, vendor-neutral, Anthropic-originated. (2) OpenAI Codex Skills and Responses-API Skills — documented as production (not beta) on developers.openai.com, with blog posts dated March 2026 reporting real usage (457 PRs merged). (5) GPT Actions in Custom GPTs — shipped and current (the "deprecated in 2024" claim surfaced in one low-quality search snippet is WRONG/unverified; the official docs page is live and describes Actions as active). PARTIALLY beta: AgentKit's Agent Builder and Connector Registry were beta at Oct 2025 DevDay launch; ChatKit + Evals were GA. DEPRECATED: Assistants API (sunset Aug 26, 2026) → migrate to Responses + Conversations API. MARKETING vs reality caveat: third-party claims of an "AgentKit 2.0" with "16 specialized agents / 40+ domain skills" (kanerika blog) are NOT corroborated by OpenAI primary sources and should be treated as low-confidence/marketing.
research.skillFormat: OpenAI Skills use the open Agent Skills format verbatim (agentskills.io/specification): a directory named to match the skill, containing a required `SKILL.md` with YAML frontmatter + Markdown body, plus optional `scripts/`, `references/`, `assets/` subdirectories. Frontmatter fields: `name` (required, max 64 chars, lowercase a-z/0-9/hyphens, must match parent dir name), `description` (required, max 1024 chars), `license` (optional), `compatibility` (optional, max 500 chars), `metadata` (optional key-value map), `allowed-tools` (optional, experimental, space-separated). OpenAI's own docs add platform constraints: exactly one SKILL.md (case-insensitive), max 25 MB uncompressed, max 500 files per version, and versioning. Codex adds an OPTIONAL OpenAI-specific file `agents/openai.yaml` for UI metadata/invocation policy/tool dependencies (interface.display_name/icon/brand_color, policy.allow_implicit_invocation, dependencies.tools). In the Responses API, skills are uploaded via `POST /v1/skills` (zip/multipart) and attached to the `shell` tool via `tools[].environment.skills` as `{"type":"skill_reference","skill_id":...,"version":N}` (or inline base64 zip). DISTINCT formats for other surfaces: function/tool calling = JSON object `{type:"function", name, description, parameters:<JSON Schema>, strict:true}` in the `tools` array; Apps SDK tools = MCP descriptors (name/title/inputSchema/outputSchema/_meta/annotations) registered via code, not files; GPT Actions = OpenAPI 3.x schema + auth config inside a Custom GPT.
research.discovery: Skills: progressive disclosure. At startup the agent/runtime injects each skill's `name`, `description`, and `path` into context (OpenAI caps this initial list at ~2% of context window / ~8,000 chars in Codex); the full SKILL.md body is loaded only when the agent decides to activate the skill, and bundled scripts/references/assets are read on demand. This is identical to Anthropic's three-stage discovery (Discovery → Activation → Execution). Function/tool calling: tools are passed explicitly in the request `tools` array; the model is told about all of them every call (no progressive disclosure). Apps SDK / MCP: ChatGPT calls the MCP server's `list_tools` (JSON-RPC 2.0 over HTTP) to enumerate tool descriptors at connection time, then the model selects based on descriptions. GPT Actions: the OpenAPI schema attached to a Custom GPT defines callable operations; the model maps natural language to endpoints via function calling under the hood.
research.interoperability: Direct and strong. OpenAI Skills ARE Anthropic Agent Skills — both implement the same agentskills.io SKILL.md spec, which agentskills.io states was "originally developed by Anthropic, released as an open standard." A SKILL.md authored for Claude Code/Claude generally runs unmodified on OpenAI Codex and vice versa; the format is the interoperability point. The carousel of adopters (Claude, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini CLI, Cursor, Copilot/VS Code, Goose, OpenHands, etc.) confirms cross-product reuse is the design goal. Adapter feasibility: trivial for skills — anymodel could ingest/emit SKILL.md directly with zero translation; only OpenAI's optional `agents/openai.yaml` and API upload/`skill_reference` wrapper are OpenAI-specific glue. For the OTHER surfaces, mapping to Agent Skills is conceptual, not structural: function/tool definitions and MCP tool descriptors map to "tools" (execution primitives), which Agent Skills can REFERENCE (via `allowed-tools` and `dependencies.tools`) but are a different layer — an adapter would treat them as the tool/MCP plane, not the skill plane. GPT Actions (OpenAPI) ↔ MCP/function tools is a known, mechanical conversion. There is no shared standard between SKILL.md and OpenAI's function-calling JSON or GPT Actions OpenAPI — those are separate concerns.
research.gapsForAnymodel: To support skills from OpenAI's ecosystem by default, anymodel needs: (1) A SKILL.md parser/loader implementing the agentskills.io spec — frontmatter validation (name/description constraints), the optional fields (license/compatibility/metadata/allowed-tools), directory resolution (scripts/references/assets), and progressive-disclosure injection (load name+description at startup with a context budget cap, lazy-load body on activation, lazy-load referenced files). This single component covers BOTH Anthropic and OpenAI since they share the format. (2) Tolerance for OpenAI's optional `agents/openai.yaml` (parse-or-ignore) so Codex-authored skills don't break. (3) An execution sandbox/shell for skill `scripts/` (container or local) — OpenAI runs skills in a shell tool with `container_auto`/`local` environments; anymodel needs an equivalent or a no-exec safe mode. (4) For API-distributed skills, an upload/reference layer mirroring `POST /v1/skills` + `skill_reference{skill_id,version}` if anymodel wants server-side skill storage; otherwise filesystem skills are enough. (5) SEPARATELY (not skills, but adjacent): an MCP client (`list_tools`/`call_tool` over JSON-RPC) and a function-calling adapter (JSON-Schema tools with strict mode) to consume OpenAI's tool/execution plane, plus optional OpenAPI→tool conversion for GPT Actions. The clean architectural insight: skills (SKILL.md, an instruction/context layer) and tools (function calling / MCP, an execution layer) are orthogonal — anymodel should implement them as two independent subsystems, with `allowed-tools`/`dependencies.tools` as the bridge.

corrections: MINOR (not a factual error, a confidence under-rating): The medium-confidence Cookbook claim about `POST /v1/skills` + `tools[].environment.skills` with `{type:'skill_reference', skill_id, version}` and `container_auto`/`local` environments is actually fully corroborated by OpenAI primary sources (developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/tools-skills, the skills_in_api cookbook, and the OpenAI API reference for the skills resource). It can be promoted to high confidence. One added detail confirmed by sources: the `version` field accepts a positive integer OR the string 'latest'.

MINOR (date precision, not a contradiction): The research labels the OpenAI skills blog as 'dated March 9, 2026' (developers.openai.com/blog/skills-agents-sdk). That post ('Using skills to accelerate OSS maintenance') does report the 457-PR figure for the Dec 1 2025-Feb 28 2026 window, which checks out. Note the separate API-skills feature ('Skills in OpenAI API') was covered by Simon Willison on Feb 11, 2026 - these are two distinct posts, so the maturity section's mix of dates is internally consistent, not contradictory.

CLARIFICATION (research already handles correctly): The 'GPT Actions deprecated in 2024' snippet still surfaces in live web search results and repeats the claim that OpenAI 'deprecated custom GPT Actions in early 2024.' This is the unreliable snippet the research correctly flags as WRONG. The official docs page (developers.openai.com/api/docs/actions/introduction) is live and describes Actions as current/active, confirming the research's stance. No correction needed - just confirming the adversarial check did not overturn the research's judgment.

CLARIFICATION (spec wording): The agentskills.io spec's progressive-disclosure stage 1 loads 'name and description' (~100 tokens); the 'path' field is an OpenAI-API-platform addition (it injects name+description+path), not part of the base agentskills.io spec. The research conflates these slightly in the discoveryMechanism section but the underlying claims are each individually correct per their respective sources.
verification.verdict:
CONFIRMED. Every concrete, falsifiable claim in this research was independently verified against live primary sources, and none could be refuted. The core thesis holds: OpenAI has no proprietary 'skills' standard and instead adopts the open Agent Skills (SKILL.md) format from agentskills.io, shipped in Codex and the Responses API. No hallucinated file formats, frontmatter fields, manifest schemas, or protocol names were found.

Verbatim confirmations against primary sources:
- agentskills.io/specification: frontmatter table matches EXACTLY - name (req, max 64, lowercase a-z/0-9/hyphens, no leading/trailing/consecutive hyphens, must match parent dir), description (req, max 1024), license (opt), compatibility (opt, max 500), metadata (opt key-value map), allowed-tools (opt, experimental, space-separated). Recommended <500 lines / <5000 tokens. skills-ref validator confirmed (github.com/agentskills/agentskills/tree/main/skills-ref). Directory layout SKILL.md/scripts/references/assets confirmed.
- agentskills.io home: attribution verbatim - 'The Agent Skills format was originally developed by Anthropic, released as an open standard.' Three-stage progressive disclosure (Discovery/Activation/Execution) confirmed verbatim. Adopter carousel includes Claude, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini CLI, Cursor, GitHub Copilot, VS Code, Goose, OpenHands (40+ total).
- developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/tools-skills: 25 MB uncompressed limit, 500 files/version, name+description+path auto-added to prompt context, 'compatible with the open Agent Skills standard', exactly one case-insensitive SKILL.md - all confirmed verbatim.
- developers.openai.com/codex/skills: optional agents/openai.yaml with interface/policy/dependencies (display_name, icon, brand_color, allow_implicit_invocation, dependencies.tools) confirmed; ~2% context / 8,000-char cap confirmed verbatim; /skills, $ mention, implicit invocation confirmed; 'builds on the open agent skills standard' confirmed.
- Responses API skills: POST /v1/skills + tools[].environment.skills + skill_reference{skill_id, version} + container_auto/local confirmed (was marked medium; can be high).
- Apps SDK MCP: _meta keys including openai/toolInvocation/invoking confirmed; registerAppTool, list_tools over JSON-RPC confirmed.
- Date-sensitive: 457 PRs (Dec 1 2025-Feb 28 2026) confirmed; Assistants API deprecated Aug 26 2025 / sunset Aug 26 2026 -> Responses+Conversations confirmed; AgentKit DevDay Oct 6 2025, ChatKit+Evals GA, Agent Builder+Connector Registry beta confirmed.
- kanerika 'AgentKit 2.0 / 16 agents / 40+ skills' correctly flagged as uncorroborated marketing; GPT Actions 'deprecated 2024' snippet correctly flagged as wrong (official docs page is live/active).

The corrections list contains only confidence upgrades and minor wording clarifications - zero substantive errors. Safe to use as-is.

sources.claim: OpenAI API skills are a versioned bundle of files + a SKILL.md manifest (frontmatter name/description + Markdown instructions); explicitly 'compatible with the open Agent Skills standard' (agentskills.io); name/description/path auto-added to prompt context; max 25MB uncompressed, 500 files/version; presented as production.

Codex Skills use SKILL.md with required frontmatter name+description; directory layout SKILL.md/scripts/references/assets plus optional agents/openai.yaml (interface/policy/dependencies); discovery via progressive disclosure capped ~2% context / 8000 chars; invoked via /skills, $ mention, or implicit description match; builds on the open agent skills standard; production-ready.

Agent Skills is an open format: a folder with SKILL.md (name+description minimum) + optional scripts/references/assets; three-stage progressive disclosure (Discovery/Activation/Execution); 'originally developed by Anthropic, released as an open standard'; adopted by Claude, Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini CLI, Cursor, Copilot/VS Code, Goose, OpenHands, and many more.

Full spec: name (req, max 64, lowercase a-z/0-9/hyphens, matches parent dir), description (req, max 1024), license (opt), compatibility (opt, max 500), metadata (opt map), allowed-tools (opt, experimental, space-separated); recommended <500 lines/<5000 tokens; validate with skills-ref.

OpenAI blog (dated March 9, 2026) describes skills as SKILL.md + scripts/references/assets with progressive disclosure; references agentskills.io/specification; reports 457 PRs merged Dec 2025-Feb 2026 using skills. Does NOT explicitly claim Claude interop in this post.

In the Responses API, skills are uploaded (POST /v1/skills) and attached to the shell tool via tools[].environment.skills as skill_reference{skill_id, optional version}, with environment type container_auto or local; inline base64 zip also supported.

Apps SDK MCP servers register tools in code (registerAppTool) exposing name/title/inputSchema/outputSchema/_meta.ui.resourceUri/_meta.ui.visibility/annotations; discovery via MCP list_tools over JSON-RPC 2.0/HTTP (no static manifest); OpenAI-specific _meta keys like openai/toolInvocation/invoking, openai/fileParams.

GPT Actions are current/shipped, tied to Custom GPTs, declared via an OpenAPI schema + auth config; they call external REST APIs by converting NL to JSON via function calling. (Contradicts a low-quality snippet claiming Actions were deprecated in 2024.)

Function/tool calling is the core OpenAI tool primitive, now on the Responses API: tools array of {type:function, name, description, parameters:<JSON Schema>}; strict:true guarantees arguments match the schema (Structured Outputs).
AgentKit launched at DevDay Oct 6, 2025 with Agent Builder (beta), Connector Registry (beta rollout), ChatKit (GA), and Evals (GA). It is an agent-building suite, not a skills format.
Assistants API was deprecated Aug 26, 2025 and sunsets/shuts down Aug 26, 2026; replacement is Responses API + Conversations API.
Third-party blog claims an 'AgentKit 2.0' with '16 specialized agents' and '40+ domain skills' — NOT corroborated by OpenAI primary sources; treat as marketing/unverified.

Exact non-name/description SKILL.md frontmatter fields on Google's side are not documented (only name+description verified).
research.skillFormat:
Multiple coexisting formats:

(1) AGENT SKILLS (SKILL.md) — the primary reusable-skill format, shared across Gemini CLI, Antigravity, and Gemini Code Assist, and explicitly the same open standard as Anthropic's. A skill is a self-contained directory:
  my-skill/
  ├── SKILL.md      (required)
  ├── scripts/      (optional)
  ├── references/   (optional)
  └── assets/       (optional)
SKILL.md uses YAML frontmatter. Confirmed fields in Google docs: `name` (unique id, should match the directory name) and `description` (CRITICAL — how the model decides when to activate; should include trigger keywords). Example:
  ---
  name: code-reviewer
  description: Expertise in reviewing code changes for correctness, security, and style.
  ---
Google's official creating-skills doc lists only name + description; it does not document additional frontmatter fields (Anthropic's own format adds optional license/allowed-tools, so portable skills may carry more — unverified on Google's side).

(2) GEMINI CLI EXTENSION (gemini-extension.json) — a package wrapper that can bundle skills, commands, context, and MCP servers. Manifest fields: `name`, `version`, `contextFileName` (defaults to GEMINI.md if present), `mcpServers` (each with `command`, `args` (supports ${extensionPath}), `cwd`, plus `serverUrl` for HTTP), `settings` (array of {name, description, envVar, sensitive}), and `excludeTools` (per the extension reference). Layout: gemini-extension.json + optional GEMINI.md + commands/<group>/<name>.toml + skills/<name>/SKILL.md.

(3) CUSTOM COMMANDS — TOML files (.toml) in a commands/ directory; fields `prompt` (supports {{args}} placeholders and !{shell} execution) and `description`; expose a /group:command slash command.

(4) ANTIGRAVITY WORKFLOWS — markdown files at .agent/workflows/<name>.md, starting with YAML frontmatter (at minimum `description`) followed by ordered markdown steps; invoked as /workflow-name in the Agent chat; a `// turbo` line above a step authorizes auto-running its command. (Note path is `.agent/workflows/` per official codelab/docs, while Antigravity Skills use `.agents/skills/` — both spellings appear in Google sources.)

(5) RULES — GEMINI.md (Antigravity/Gemini-native, project root or .gemini/) and AGENTS.md (cross-tool, takes precedence; global ~/.gemini/AGENTS.md, added to Antigravity ~v1.20.3, March 2026); .agent/rules/ for split rule files.

(6) GEMS — NOT a file format. A Gem is a saved config object in the Gemini consumer app: Name, Description, Instructions (system prompt), optional Default tool, optional Knowledge files (incl. live Google Drive). Created/edited in the app UI; shareable Private/Shared/Public.

(7) A2A AgentSkill — JSON object inside an AgentCard (not a file bundle). Required: `id`, `name`, `description`, `tags` (string[]); optional: `examples` (string[]), `inputModes`, `outputModes` (string[] media types).
research.discovery:
Skill (SKILL.md) discovery in Gemini CLI / Code Assist / Antigravity is tiered filesystem scanning at session start: built-in skills → extension skills → user skills (~/.gemini/skills/ or the ~/.agents/skills/ alias) → workspace skills (.gemini/skills/ or the .agents/skills/ alias). Within a tier, the .agents/skills/ alias takes precedence over .gemini/skills/ — this alias is the deliberate cross-tool interoperability path. SKILL.md is found at the skills-dir root or one level deep (<skill-name>/SKILL.md). Antigravity CLI additionally reads ~/.gemini/antigravity-cli/skills, and both Antigravity CLI and IDE share ~/.gemini/skills and ~/.gemini/config/mcp_config.json (per a May 2026 Google Cloud community write-up).

At session start only each skill's name + description are injected into the system prompt (progressive disclosure). Antigravity Workflows are discovered by scanning .agent/workflows/*.md and registered as /<name> slash commands. Gemini CLI extensions are discovered from installed/linked extension directories (gemini extensions link/new). Gems are discovered through the Gemini app's Gems panel and a public Gems gallery (10,000+ public Gems reported by March 2026). A2A agents are discovered by fetching the AgentCard at the well-known URL /.well-known/agent-card.json (earlier v0.2.x used /.well-known/agent.json); v1.0 adds cryptographically signed Agent Cards for cross-org trust.
research.interoperability:
Strong, deliberate interoperability with Anthropic Agent Skills at the SKILL.md layer. Google's docs and Google Cloud community material state skills are based on the open Agent Skills standard (agentskills.io / skills.sh) "originally proposed by Anthropic," and that a skill installed in Gemini CLI works the same in Claude Code (~/.claude/skills/), Codex CLI, Cursor, and Copilot. The shared neutral path .agents/skills/ (and ~/.agents/skills/) is the explicit cross-tool convention; Gemini CLI/Code Assist/Antigravity treat it as a first-class alias that overrides the vendor-specific .gemini/skills/. AGENTS.md plays the analogous role for rules across tools. Practical consequence: an Anthropic-format skill folder (SKILL.md + scripts/references/assets) is portable into Google's tools today with little to no change, and vice versa — adapter feasibility is essentially "drop-in" for the core name+description+body; only richer/optional frontmatter fields risk being ignored by Google's loader (Google docs only document name+description).

MCP: orthogonal and fully supported by Google's tools (gemini-extension.json mcpServers, Antigravity mcp_config.json), same as Anthropic — MCP supplies tools/data, Skills supply procedural knowledge; they compose.

A2A: maps to neither Anthropic Skills nor MCP directly. It is a remote-agent interop protocol; its AgentSkill (id/name/description/tags/examples/inputModes/outputModes) is a capability advertisement for delegation, comparable conceptually to an MCP tool/server card more than to a SKILL.md bundle. An adapter could surface A2A AgentSkills as callable tools, but A2A does not package reusable instructions/prompts the way SKILL.md does.

Gems do not interoperate via files — they are a closed consumer-app config object with no documented export/import format, so no clean adapter to SKILL.md.
research.gapsForAnymodel:
To support Google-ecosystem skills by default, anymodel would need:

1) A SKILL.md loader matching the shared open standard: parse YAML frontmatter (at minimum name + description), implement progressive disclosure (inject only name+description at session start, load the full body + bundled scripts/references/assets only on activation), and an activate-skill step with optional user confirmation. This is the single highest-leverage piece and is the same format Anthropic Agent Skills use — one loader covers Claude Code, Gemini CLI, Antigravity, Code Assist, Cursor, Codex, Copilot.

2) Multi-path discovery with the cross-tool alias: scan ~/.agents/skills and .agents/skills (neutral, highest interop value) plus vendor paths (~/.gemini/skills, .gemini/skills, ~/.claude/skills). Honor the precedence rule (.agents/skills overrides vendor path within a tier) and the root-or-one-level-deep SKILL.md placement.

3) AGENTS.md support as the cross-tool always-on rules file (plus reading GEMINI.md/CLAUDE.md when present), and .agent/rules/ split files.

4) Antigravity Workflow support: parse .agent/workflows/*.md (YAML frontmatter + markdown steps), register them as /name slash commands, and recognize the // turbo auto-run authorization marker.

5) Gemini CLI extension packaging: read gemini-extension.json (name, version, contextFileName, mcpServers, settings, excludeTools) and TOML custom commands (commands/<group>/<name>.toml with prompt/{{args}}/!{shell}/description) so extension-bundled skills load too.

6) MCP client parity (already largely standard) — Google tools rely on MCP for tools/data via mcp_config.json / mcpServers; anymodel should configure servers the same way so Skills+MCP compose.

7) A2A is a separate track, only if anymodel acts as or calls remote agents: implement AgentCard publishing/consumption at /.well-known/agent-card.json (and legacy /.well-known/agent.json), the AgentSkill schema (id/name/description/tags/examples/inputModes/outputModes), and v1.0 signed-card verification. This is interop with remote agents, not skill packaging, and should be kept architecturally distinct from the SKILL.md path.

8) Gems have no file/export format, so there is nothing to adapt — anymodel can ignore Gems for portability and instead treat its own profiles/system-prompts as the equivalent.

Recommended priority: SKILL.md + .agents/skills discovery (1–2) first, then AGENTS.md (3), then Antigravity workflows + extensions (4–5); A2A (7) only if remote-agent interop is a goal.

corrections: ANTIGRAVITY SKILLS PATH IS WRONG (material). Research repeatedly asserts Antigravity Skills live at '.agents/skills/<name>/SKILL.md' (plural) and rates it high-confidence. The official Google codelab 'Getting Started with Antigravity Skills' explicitly uses '.agent/skills/' (SINGULAR) for workspace scope and '~/.gemini/antigravity/skills/' for global scope. The '.agents/skills/' (plural) is the Gemini CLI / Code Assist cross-tool ALIAS, not Antigravity's native skills path. The research conflates the two. Antigravity Workflows are correctly '.agent/workflows/' (singular) and the research notes 'both spellings appear' — but it picked the wrong spelling for Skills.

ANTIGRAVITY GLOBAL SKILLS PATH UNVERIFIED/LIKELY WRONG. Research cites '~/.gemini/skills/' (from a Medium write-up) as Antigravity's shared skills path. The official codelab says global Antigravity skills live at '~/.gemini/antigravity/skills/'. The '~/.gemini/skills/' path for Antigravity is not confirmed by any primary source.

gemini-extension.json 'serverUrl' (HTTP MCP) field is UNVERIFIED. The official extension reference (geminicli.com/docs/extensions/reference) documents mcpServers with command/args/cwd but does NOT list a 'serverUrl' or 'httpUrl' field for HTTP transport in extension-bundled MCP servers. Flag as unconfirmed; the rest of gemini-extension.json (name, version, description, contextFileName, mcpServers, excludeTools, settings{name,description,envVar,sensitive}, ${extensionPath}) IS confirmed.

SKILL name source nuance: research says name 'should match the directory name.' The actual Gemini CLI / Code Assist loader takes the skill name from the 'name:' frontmatter field, NOT the directory; directory-match is only a recommendation. Minor.

OPEN-STANDARD ATTRIBUTION nuance: Google's own primary docs cite the standard as 'agentskills.io' (and skills.sh) and call it an open standard, but do NOT in the primary doc explicitly credit 'Anthropic'. The 'originally proposed by Anthropic' framing comes from secondary/community sources only. The summary slightly overstates this as Google's own positioning.

Lower-confidence items correctly self-flagged remain unverified against primary Google sources: AGENTS.md-added-to-Antigravity-in-v1.20.3 (March 2026), '.agent/rules/' split-rules dir, the May 2026 community claims about '~/.gemini/antigravity-cli/skills' and a Go-based 'Antigravity CLI', and the 'Antigravity 2.0' framework (buildmvpfast). Keep these flagged.
verification.verdict: Core format and discovery claims VERIFIED against primary sources — no hallucinated formats. Every named construct (SKILL.md + YAML frontmatter name/description, progressive disclosure via activate_skill with user confirmation, gemini-extension.json, TOML custom commands with {{args}}/!{shell}, .agent/workflows/*.md with // turbo, GEMINI.md/AGENTS.md/mcp_config.json, A2A AgentCard at /.well-known/agent-card.json with AgentSkill{id,name,description,tags,examples,inputModes,outputModes}, v1.0 early-2026 signed cards / LF donation June 2025, Gems as UI-config-not-file) corresponds to a real, confirmed construct. Gemini CLI discovery paths (~/.gemini/skills + ~/.agents/skills alias; .gemini/skills + .agents/skills alias; alias precedence; agentskills.io standard) verified verbatim against geminicli.com and google-gemini/gemini-cli GitHub. ONE MATERIAL ERROR: the Antigravity Skills directory is '.agent/skills/' (singular) + '~/.gemini/antigravity/skills/' (global) per the official codelab, NOT '.agents/skills/' as the research states with high confidence — the research conflated Antigravity's native skills path with the Gemini CLI cross-tool alias. Fix that path before treating this research as authoritative. Plus minor over-claims: gemini-extension.json serverUrl field unverified, name-matches-directory is a recommendation not a rule, and the Anthropic-authorship framing is secondary not primary.

sources.claim: Gemini CLI ships Agent Skills (SKILL.md) with progressive disclosure (only name+description loaded initially; activate_skill tool with user confirmation), discovered from built-in/extension/user(~/.gemini/skills or ~/.agents/skills)/workspace(.gemini/skills or .agents/skills) tiers; based on the open Agent Skills standard.
SKILL.md uses YAML frontmatter with name and description; recommended layout SKILL.md + scripts/ + references/ + assets/; description is critical for activation.

Custom commands are TOML files in commands/ with prompt (supports {{args}} and !{shell}) and description, exposed as /group:command.
Both Claude Code and Gemini Code Assist discover custom skills in the .agents/skills/ directory, making skills portable across ecosystems.

Antigravity uses .agents/skills/<name>.md skill files and .agents/workflows/<name>.md workflow files with YAML frontmatter (description); saving a file in .agents/workflows/ registers a /slash command in the chat.

Antigravity Skills (.agents/skills/<name>/SKILL.md) = what the agent knows; Workflows = how it executes, invoked via /deploy-style slash commands; workflows call skills.
Antigravity shares skills at ~/.gemini/skills/<name>/SKILL.md and MCP config at ~/.gemini/config/mcp_config.json across IDE and CLI; CLI also reads ~/.gemini/antigravity-cli/skills (May 24 2026).

research.maturity: SHIPPED, not speculative — but young and lightly governed. MCP: stable, mature open protocol (announced Nov 2024 by Anthropic; spec dated 2025-06-18 with tools/resources/prompts as first-class primitives at modelcontextprotocol.io); broad multi-vendor adoption. Agent Skills / SKILL.md: real and widely shipped, but the *standardization* is recent and de-facto rather than committee-driven. Anthropic shipped skills in 2025; OpenAI Codex/ChatGPT added support ~Nov-Dec 2025 (Simon Willison documented it Dec 12 2025 and called it "a very light specification"); the format was published as an "open standard" at the vendor-neutral agentskills.io with a written specification, validator (skills-ref), and client-implementation guide. The reported "Dec 18 2025 release date" and the "32 tools" / "three labs converged independently" framing come from secondary blog sources and should be treated as approximate marketing-grade claims, not a dated normative milestone (confidence medium). AGENTS.md: mature as a convention (OpenAI, Aug 2025; now under Linux Foundation's Agentic AI Foundation; 60k+ repos) but it is explicitly a project-instructions file, NOT a skill format. Bottom line: the skill-portability story is real shipped reality as of early 2026, but it's a lightweight de-facto convergence on a directory+Markdown format, not a heavyweight ratified standard with formal versioning/conformance.
research.skillFormat: Agent Skills (SKILL.md) format, per the official spec at agentskills.io/specification: a skill is a directory whose name matches the skill `name`, containing a required `SKILL.md` file plus optional `scripts/` (executable code), `references/` (docs loaded on demand), and `assets/` (templates/data) subdirectories. SKILL.md = YAML frontmatter between `---` delimiters + Markdown body. Frontmatter fields: `name` (REQUIRED; 1-64 chars, lowercase a-z/0-9/hyphens, no leading/trailing/consecutive hyphens, must match parent dir name), `description` (REQUIRED; 1-1024 chars, must state what the skill does AND when to use it), `license` (optional), `compatibility` (optional, ≤500 chars, environment/product requirements), `metadata` (optional arbitrary string→string map, used for author/version), `allowed-tools` (optional, experimental, space-separated pre-approved tools e.g. `Bash(git:*) Read`). Body is free-form Markdown (recommended <500 lines / <5000 tokens; split overflow into reference files). VENDOR DIVERGENCE exists at the edges: OpenAI Codex uses the same SKILL.md (name+description) but puts client-specific metadata in a sidecar `agents/openai.yaml` (interface.display_name, icons, policy.allow_implicit_invocation, dependencies.tools). Anthropic ships some skills (docx/pdf/pptx/xlsx) as source-available, not Apache-2.0. Note: GEMINI.md / CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md are project-context instruction files, a DIFFERENT artifact class from skills.
research.discovery: Discovery is filesystem-directory scanning at session start (for local agents). The agentskills.io client-implementation guide prescribes scanning project-level and user-level scopes, each at both a client-native path and the cross-client `.agents/skills/` convention: project `<proj>/.<client>/skills/` + `<proj>/.agents/skills/`; user `~/.<client>/skills/` + `~/.agents/skills/`. Concrete per-tool paths: Claude Code `~/.claude/skills/` + `.claude/skills/`; Codex `$CWD/.agents/skills`, ancestors up to repo root, `$HOME/.agents/skills`, `/etc/codex/skills`, plus bundled system skills (`.system`); Gemini CLI `~/.gemini/skills/` + `.gemini/skills/`, with `.agents/skills/` taking precedence within a tier. Many clients also pragmatically scan `.claude/skills/` since the largest existing skill corpus lives there. The spec itself does NOT mandate directory locations — it only defines SKILL.md contents — so `.agents/skills/` is a de-facto community convention, not a normative requirement. Scanning rules: skip `.git`/`node_modules`, optionally honor `.gitignore`, bound depth (~4-6) and dir count (~2000). Precedence on name collision: project overrides user (universal convention); trust-gate project-level skills against prompt-injection from untrusted repos. The runtime decides to *trigger* a skill via the model's own judgment matching the task to the loaded name+description (model-driven), not harness keyword matching.
research.interoperability: Strong and largely SOLVED for the skill layer; a single universal loader is feasible and already documented. SKILL.md is one shared format across Claude Code, Codex/ChatGPT, Gemini CLI, Copilot/VS Code, Cursor, Goose, Junie, Kiro, Roo Code, Mistral Vibe, and ~30 others, all reading from `.agents/skills/` (+ client-native dirs). A loader needs only: (1) multi-directory scan with project>user precedence; (2) split YAML frontmatter / Markdown body on `---`; (3) lenient parsing — the official guide warns skills authored for other clients often have technically-invalid-but-tolerated YAML (e.g., unquoted `description: Use when: ...` with a colon) and recommends a quote-and-retry fallback, warn-don't-fail on name-length/dir-mismatch, skip only on missing description or unparseable YAML. CAVEATS for a universal loader: (a) it captures the SKILL.md commonality but NOT vendor sidecar metadata — Codex's `agents/openai.yaml` (icons, implicit-invocation policy, tool deps) would be ignored or need per-vendor adapters; (b) `allowed-tools`/`dependencies.tools` semantics vary per client and aren't portable as-executed; (c) GEMINI.md/CLAUDE.md/AGENTS.md project-instruction files are a different artifact and out of scope. MAPPING TO MCP: an MCP "prompt" (name/description/arguments → templated messages) is the closest protocol analog to a skill and a SKILL.md *could* be wrapped as an MCP prompt or resource to serve it over the wire, but the mapping is lossy (skills bundle executable scripts and on-demand reference files; an MCP prompt returns only message content). No official Skill↔MCP-prompt bridge spec exists; it would be a custom adapter. AGENTS.md has no skill mapping at all.
research.gapsForAnymodel: To support ecosystem skills by default, anymodel would need: (1) A SKILL.md loader implementing the three-tier progressive-disclosure model — scan project + user scopes across `.agents/skills/`, `.claude/skills/`, `.codex/skills/`, `.gemini/skills/`; parse `---`-delimited YAML frontmatter (name, description required; license/compatibility/metadata/allowed-tools optional) + Markdown body; project>user collision precedence; lenient YAML parsing with quote-and-retry fallback for cross-client malformed values; trust-gating of project-level skills against prompt injection. (2) A catalog injector that emits name+description (~50-100 tokens each) into the system prompt or an `activate_skill` tool description, capped to a context budget. (3) An activation path (file-read tool OR dedicated tool that strips frontmatter, wraps in identifying tags, enumerates bundled resources without eager-loading) plus an explicit `/skill-name` slash-command path. (4) Context-management: exempt activated skill content from compaction/pruning; dedupe re-activations. (5) Per-vendor ADAPTERS for the non-portable edges: read Codex's `agents/openai.yaml` sidecar, map client-specific `allowed-tools`/tool-dependency declarations, and handle source-available (non-OSS) bundled skills like docx/pdf/pptx/xlsx. (6) OPTIONAL MCP bridge: expose loaded skills as MCP prompts/resources (lossy — scripts and reference files don't map cleanly) and/or consume MCP prompts as pseudo-skills, since no official Skill↔MCP adapter exists. (7) Keep AGENTS.md handling as a SEPARATE project-instructions ingester (it is not a skill). Net: ~80% of portability is a straightforward shared SKILL.md loader; the remaining ~20% is per-vendor metadata sidecars and execution-permission semantics that genuinely differ and require adapters.

verification.target: mcp-and-portability research (Model Context Protocol & cross-ecosystem skill portability, early 2026)
corrections: NOT a correction — a confirmation note: every high-risk concrete claim was independently verified against the live primary source. agentskills.io/specification confirms the exact SKILL.md frontmatter fields (name required <=64 chars lowercase-alphanumeric-hyphens no leading/trailing/consecutive hyphens must match parent dir; description required <=1024 chars; license/compatibility<=500/metadata/allowed-tools optional+experimental) and the scripts//references//assets/ subdirs verbatim.

The .agents/skills/ cross-client convention and the client-implementation guide at agentskills.io/client-implementation/adding-skills-support are real and match the research almost word-for-word: project>user precedence, three-tier catalog(~50-100 tok)/instructions(<5000 tok)/resources loading, lenient YAML with quote-and-retry fallback for unquoted-colon descriptions, skip .git/node_modules, depth/dir-count bounds, trust-gating of project skills, activate_skill dedicated-tool pattern with <skill_content> wrapping, and compaction-exemption. The spec explicitly does NOT mandate directory locations (de-facto convention) — research correctly states this.

Codex sidecar is REAL and exactly named agents/openai.yaml at developers.openai.com/codex/skills, with the claimed structure (interface.display_name/icons/etc., policy.allow_implicit_invocation, dependencies.tools) and the ~2%-of-context / 8000-char cap, and it cites agentskills.io as the open standard. This was the single highest hallucination-risk claim (very specific filename+schema) and it is confirmed.

MCP prompts confirmed at modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/prompts: user-controlled message templates with name/title/description/arguments, discovered via prompts/list, fetched via prompts/get, distinct from model-controlled tools and from resources; spec dated 2025-06-18. Correct.

AGENTS.md confirmed at agents.md: tool-agnostic 'README for agents' project-instructions format, stewarded by the Agentic AI Foundation under the Linux Foundation, used by 60k+ open-source projects, covers build/test/style; contains NO skill definition. Research's insistence that AGENTS.md is a separate artifact class is correct.

Gemini CLI (geminicli.com/docs/cli/skills) confirmed: SKILL.md skills at ~/.gemini/skills/ and .gemini/skills/ with .agents/skills/ alias taking precedence within a tier; GEMINI.md explicitly distinguished as persistent context, not a skill. Anthropic repo (github.com/anthropics/skills) confirmed: points to agentskills.io as the standard, most skills Apache-2.0 but docx/pdf/pptx/xlsx are explicitly 'source-available, not open source', Notion partner skill listed.

Simon Willison post (2025/Dec/12) confirmed verbatim: documents OpenAI skills in ChatGPT+Codex resembling anthropic/skills, calls it 'a very light specification, if you could even call it that', and suggests the Agentic AI Foundation formally document it. Research's framing of de-facto-convergence-not-ratification is accurate.

Minor caveats (do not affect the verdict): (1) The research's own self-flagged medium/low-confidence items — the secondary-source '32 tools', the 'Dec 18 2025 release date', and the 'three labs converged INDEPENDENTLY' narrative — remain the only soft spots; the research already correctly downgrades these to marketing-grade/interpretive, so no over-claim exists. The real adopter list on agentskills.io is in fact larger than ~30 (40+ clients now listed, including Junie, Cursor, Goose, OpenCode, OpenHands, Spring AI, Laravel Boost, etc.), so '~30+' if anything understates breadth. (2) 'Kiro' is confirmed as an adopter (kiro.dev/docs/skills). (3) No official Skill<->MCP-prompt bridge spec was found, consistent with the research's claim that any such adapter would be custom.
verification.verdict: CONFIRMED. This research is accurate and unusually well-sourced — no hallucinated file formats, frontmatter fields, manifest schemas, or protocol names. I attempted to refute the highest-risk concrete claims by fetching each primary source live, and every one held up: (1) agentskills.io and its /specification exist, with SKILL.md frontmatter fields and constraints matching verbatim (name/description required, license/compatibility/metadata/allowed-tools optional; scripts//references//assets/ subdirs). (2) The .agents/skills/ cross-client convention and the official client-implementation guide are real and describe the three-tier progressive-disclosure loader, project>user precedence, and lenient quote-and-retry YAML parsing exactly as stated — making the 'single universal loader is feasible and documented' claim correct. (3) The single most hallucination-prone claim — Codex's sidecar file named agents/openai.yaml with policy.allow_implicit_invocation / dependencies.tools and a ~2%/8000-char context cap — is REAL and confirmed at developers.openai.com. (4) MCP prompts (user-controlled, prompts/list + prompts/get, spec 2025-06-18), (5) AGENTS.md under the Agentic AI Foundation/Linux Foundation with 60k+ repos and explicitly NOT a skill format, (6) Gemini CLI paths with .agents/skills precedence and GEMINI.md as separate context, (7) Anthropic's source-available docx/pdf/pptx/xlsx skills, and (8) the Simon Willison 'very light specification' quote all verified against their cited URLs. The core format and discovery claims fully hold. The only soft spots are ones the research itself already flags as medium/low confidence (secondary-source '32 tools', a specific Dec-18 release date, and the 'independent convergence' narrative), so there is no over-claiming. Recommend shipping as-is; the gapsForAnymodel engineering plan rests on a factually sound foundation.

sources.claim: Official Agent Skills spec: skill = directory with required SKILL.md (YAML frontmatter: name + description required; license/compatibility/metadata/allowed-tools optional) plus optional scripts/, references/, assets/; field constraints (name ≤64 chars lowercase, description ≤1024 chars).

Agent Skills format originated at Anthropic, released as an open standard, stewarded at vendor-neutral agentskills.io; adopter list includes Claude Code, OpenAI Codex, Gemini CLI, GitHub Copilot, VS Code, Cursor, Goose, JetBrains Junie, Kiro, Roo Code, Mistral Vibe and ~30 others with real doc URLs; defines progressive disclosure (discovery/activation/execution).

Official guide for building a skills loader: scan project+user scopes at `.<client>/skills/` and the cross-client `.agents/skills/` convention; project overrides user on name collisions; lenient YAML parsing with quote-and-retry for cross-client malformed frontmatter; three-tier catalog/instructions/resources loading. Directly establishes feasibility of a universal loader.

MCP prompts are user-controlled reusable message templates (name/title/description/arguments) discovered via prompts/list and fetched via prompts/get, distinct from model-controlled tools and from resources; spec dated 2025-06-18.

Anthropic's repo positions itself as its *implementation* of skills for Claude and points to agentskills.io as the authoritative standard; many skills Apache-2.0, but docx/pdf/pptx/xlsx are source-available not OSS; references Partner Skills (e.g. Notion).

OpenAI Codex uses SKILL.md (name+description required), scans `.agents/skills` from CWD up through repo root plus `$HOME/.agents/skills` and `/etc/codex/skills`; puts client-specific metadata in sidecar `agents/openai.yaml`; states it builds on the open agentskills.io standard; initial skills list capped ~2% of context.

Gemini CLI supports SKILL.md skills (name+description required, frontmatter must be first; silently skipped if malformed) stored at ~/.gemini/skills/ and .gemini/skills/, with `.agents/skills/` taking precedence within a tier; GEMINI.md is a separate persistent-context file, not a skill.

On 12 Dec 2025, Simon Willison documented OpenAI quietly adopting skills in ChatGPT and Codex CLI, noting they look very similar to Anthropic's anthropic/skills implementation and calling it 'a very light specification' that the Agentic AI Foundation should formally document — i.e. de-facto convergence, not formal ratification.

AGENTS.md is a tool-agnostic standard Markdown file of project-level instructions for coding agents (a 'README for agents'), stewarded by the Agentic AI Foundation under the Linux Foundation, adopted by 60k+ repos; it covers build/test/style/conventions and does NOT define skills.

Secondary source claiming the Agent Skills open standard was adopted across 32 tools and a specific late-Dec-2025 release; useful corroboration of breadth but the exact date/count are marketing-grade, not primary-verified.

Secondary source framing 'three labs (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind) independently converged on nearly the same skill format.' The convergence is real and primary-confirmed via vendor docs, but the 'independent, no coordination' narrative is interpretive, not authoritative.
